name: Pan
description: Pan, in Greek mythology, is the rustic god of the wild, shepherds, flocks, goatherds, and of mountain wilds, music, and impromptus. He is often depicted as having the hindquarters, legs, and horns of a goat, mirroring his connection to the animal world and the untamed wilderness. Pan's origins are somewhat obscure, with varying accounts of his parentage, though he is commonly associated with Hermes. He is known for his playful and often mischievous nature, frequently pursuing nymphs and engaging in revelry with other woodland deities. His presence evokes both joy and fear; the sudden, unreasoning fear that overtakes individuals in lonely places is termed 'panic' in his honor. Pan's music, played on his syrinx or panpipes, is both enchanting and unsettling, reflecting the dual nature of the wild he embodies. He is a liminal figure, existing on the boundary between civilization and the untamed, representing the primal and instinctive aspects of human nature.
subtitle: God of the Wild

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Panpipes
keyMyths.description: Pan's most famous attribute is his syrinx, or panpipes. The myth recounts his pursuit of the nymph Syrinx, who, to escape his advances, transformed herself into reeds. Heartbroken, Pan fashioned the reeds into a musical instrument, forever bearing her name. This story highlights Pan's connection to nature and his ability to transform loss into art. It also emphasizes the inherent longing and unfulfilled desire often associated with the wild.

keyMyths.title: Pan and Echo
keyMyths.description: Another tragic tale involves the nymph Echo, known for her beautiful voice. Depending on the version, either Pan fell in love with Echo and she rejected him, or she was cursed by Hera to only repeat the words of others. In the former version, Pan, in a fit of rage, drove local herdsmen mad, and they tore Echo to pieces, scattering her remains. This myth illustrates the destructive potential of unrequited love and Pan's capacity for violent emotion.

keyMyths.title: Pan's Assistance in Battle
keyMyths.description: During the Battle of Marathon, Pan is said to have aided the Athenians by instilling panic into the Persian army. This sudden, irrational fear caused confusion and disarray among the invaders, contributing to the Athenian victory. This myth demonstrates Pan's power to influence human affairs and his role as a protector of the wild and the innocent. It also solidifies the association of the word 'panic' with the god himself.
